content.full_text:
GRUENEICH & ELLISON

ATTORNEYS AT LAW

April 26, 1990


Kathryn Donovan, General Counsel
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 "J" Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Request for Advice

Dear Ms. Donovan:

By this letter, I request advice on behalf of my firm and
several of my firm's clients related to lobbying registration and
reporting requirements under the Political Reform Act and related
FPPC regulations (collectively referred to herein as the "Act").

I. California Energy Commission Activities:

(A) My firm represents two national nonprofit environmental
organizations in several proceedings before the California Energy
Commission ("CEC"). Several of these proceedings will result in
the CEC's adoption of reports related to State energy policy -- the
Biennial Report, the Fuels Report, the Electricity Report and the
Conservation Report.¹ In two other proceedings, the CEC will
consider modifications of the State's building and appliance
efficiency standards contained in Title 24 of the California Code
of Regulations ("CCR").

Questions:

(1) Assuming that my firm otherwise meets the
Compensation Test or Contacts Test,² does representation of these

¹These reports are used to establish the State's energy
policy. However, there is no clear statutory directive that the
findings and conclusions of these reports are implemented or
followed by other agencies or even by the CEC itself.

²The following may be relevant: My firm is a registered
lobbying firm and I am a registered lobbyist for a completely
separate client for which I provide legislative advocacy.
Additionally, one of the two environmental organizations mentioned
above is a registered lobbyist employer, but not of my firm or me.

380 HAYES STREET SUITE FOUR SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94102 TELEPHONE: 415.861.6930 TELEFAX: 415.861.0461
DIAN M. GRUENEICH BARRY H. EPSTEIN

1121 L STREET SUITE 1000 SACRAMENTO, CA 95814 TELEPHONE: 916.449.9971 TELEFAX: 916.449.9972
CHRISTOPHER T. ELLISON

Kathryn Donovan, General Counsel
April 26, 1990
Page Two

organizations in each of the above-described proceedings require
registration and disclosure under the Act (or, for example, are
only the two proceedings which may result in changes in State
regulations within the ambit of reportable activity)?

(2) If the answer to Question (1) is yes, as to any of
the CEC proceedings, even if the level of my firm's activity on
behalf of these organizations does not meet the Contacts or
Compensation Test:

(a) must my firm amend its Lobbying Firm
Registration Statement to add the two organizations?

(b) must the two organizations file a Lobbyist Firm
Activity Authorization (Form 602) naming my firm to carry out the
above-described CEC activities?

(c) must my firm and the two organizations report
as payments to my firm the amount my firm receives?

(B) Participation by these two organizations in the above-
described proceedings takes place under the CEC's Intervenor
Funding Program established by the Legislature. (A copy of SB 283,
Chapter 1436, Stats. of 1988, which established this program and a
copy of the CEC's Guidelines for this program are attached.) Under
that Program, the organizations are compensated by the CEC (using
public funds) for their participation in the proceeding, first by
obtaining the CEC's pre-approval of the budget for such
participation, and then by filing a request for compensation after
participation has been completed. The compensation covers the
actual cost of attorney and consultant fees and expenses for
representation and is awarded by the CEC at a public hearing on the
compensation request. Moreover, the organizations have instructed
the CEC to pay the compensation directly to the attorneys and
consultants, so that the funds never actually are received by the
organizations. The only compensation my firm will receive for CEC-
related activities on behalf of these two organizations will be
these public funds awarded through this process.

Question:

(3) Given that all the compensation received by my firm
consists of public money awarded by the CEC (but assuming that
either the Compensation or Contacts Test is otherwise met), does
the fact that the compensation comes from the CEC under the
Intervenor Funding Program affect the applicability of the Act's

Kathryn Donovan, General Counsel
April 26, 1990
Page Three

registration or reporting requirements (i.e., does this
compensation situation affect your answers to the Questions (1) and
(2) posed in sub-part (A), above)?

II. California Public Utilities Commission Activities:

My firm represents the Department of General Services ("DGS"),
a State agency, as outside counsel before the California Public
Utilities Commission ("CPUC"). This representation includes: (i)
participation in rate setting cases for individual regulated
utilities (e.g., a proceeding in which PG&E is seeking a rate
increase), (ii) participation in CPUC-initiated policy making
proceedings which result in CPUC orders setting forth CPUC policy
(but do not result in formal regulations in the CCR), (iii)
participation in proceedings which result in the development of
CPUC regulations and guidelines, and (iv) monitoring energy
legislation (but no advocacy).

My firm also represents a unit of the University of California
before the CPUC, engaging in the same type of activities as
described above for DGS.

Questions:

(4) Were my firm's client to be a private party, which
types of the above-described proceedings and activities (rate cases, policy proceedings,
regulation/guideline proceedings, and
legislation monitoring) fall within the Act's disclosure
requirements?

(5) Does the fact that the lobbyist-employer is the DGS,
a State agency -- or relatedly, does the fact that all the
compensation to my firm constitutes a direct expenditure of public
funds -- affect the Act's disclosure requirements? If not, does
the DGS have to file a Lobbying Firm Activity Authorization (Form
602) and file quarterly reports as a lobbyist-employer (Form 635)
covering my firm's activities?

(6) Does the fact that the lobbyist-employer is a unit
of the University of California, which holds a slightly different
status in State government than do executive branch agencies such
as DGS -- or relatedly, does the fact that all the compensation
to my firm constitutes a direct expenditure of public university funds
-- affect the Act's disclosure requirements? If not, does the
University of California have to file a Lobbying Firm Activity

Kathryn Donovan, General Counsel
April 26, 1990
Page Four

Authorization (Form 602) and file quarterly reports as a lobbyist-
employer (Form 635) covering my firm's activities?

* * *

I realize that these are complex questions for which reading
of the Act's language alone may not provide a simple answer and
which therefore may instead require consideration of the policy
behind the Act's requirements. Your timely answer would be
appreciated, as some activities described above have already
occurred and may have to be reported on filings as of April 30,
depending on your advice.

Please feel free to telephone me at 415/861-6930 if you need
additional information to completely analyze the questions
presented. Also, if you feel a summary of your response can be
provided by telephone, please do so (although the provision of your
advice in writing is ultimately desired). Finally, your written
response can be provided by facsimile to 415/861-0461.

Thank you for your assistance.

Sincerely,

Barry H. Epstein

enclosure

If not, does the DGS have to file a Lobbying Firm Activity Authorization (Form 602) and file quarterly reports as a lobbyist-employer (Form 635) covering my firm's activities?

(6) Does the fact that the lobbyist-employer is a unit of the University of California, which holds a slightly different status in State government than do executive branch agencies such as DGS -- or relatedly, does the fact that all the compensation to my firm constitutes a direct expenditure of public University funds -- affect the Act's disclosure requirements? If not, does the University of California have to file a Lobbying Firm Activity

Kathryn Donovan, General Counsel  
April 26, 1990  
Page Four

Authorization (Form 602) and file quarterly reports as a lobbyist-employer (Form 635) covering my firm's activities?
